name: Forced continuity
summary: Free trial or introductory offer silently converts to paid subscription with no clear notice or explicit consent at the moment of conversion.

evidence.claim: Brignull's canonical taxonomy lists 'forced continuity' as automatic conversion from free trial to paid subscription without explicit consent.

The federal FTC Click-to-Cancel Rule (16 CFR Part 425), which would have required explicit consent to continue after a free trial, was vacated by the 8th Circuit on July 8, 2025. State laws including CA SB-313 remain in force.

body.description: A free trial or discounted introductory period silently transitions to a paid recurring subscription. The user was not given an explicit opt-in at the moment of conversion (E1). Many jurisdictions require explicit consent at the conversion moment; federal US requirement is currently vacated (E2) but state-level requirements apply.

detectionHeuristics.description: Free trial flows that require credit card upfront are disproportionately associated with forced continuity.

body.llmVerifyPrompt: You are evaluating a signup or free-trial flow for forced continuity. The user is signing up for a trial; check whether (a) a payment method is required at signup, (b) the trial auto-converts to paid, (c) the conversion moment has an explicit prompt that requires affirmative consent, or only a notification. Return: {hasForcedContinuity: boolean, trialLengthDays: number, autoChargeAmountUsd: number, requiresExplicitConversionConsent: boolean}.
body.remediation: 1. Warn the user that this trial will auto-charge. 2. Offer to schedule a cancellation reminder 2-3 days before the trial ends. 3. Draft a pre-emptive cancellation note for the user to send if they want to try the service without the auto-charge risk.
